title: Why your nervous system treats uncertainty as danger
author: Kalesh
excerpt:

metaDescription:
You're not broken. Your body is just trying to keep you alive. 

 I remember sitting in my therapist's office, hands gripping the armrests of a chair that...
htmlContent:
You're not broken. Your body is just trying to keep you alive.

I remember sitting in my therapist's office, hands gripping the armrests of a chair that suddenly felt way too small. She asked me a simple question about my weekend plans. And my mind went completely blank. My chest tightened. My throat closed up. I couldn't speak.

Not because I was hiding something. Not because I was being difficult. But because my nervous system had just hit the panic button over a question about Saturday morning coffee.

Here's the thing. I spent years believing I was defective. That other people somehow got a manual for life that I'd missed. That everyone else could handle the normal uncertainty of daily existence while I crumbled at the slightest curveball. A cancelled meeting. A text left on read. A change in plans that most people would shrug off and I'd spend the next three days replaying in a loop.

But here's what I've learned, and what the research keeps showing us: your nervous system isn't designed to handle uncertainty. It's designed to survive. And those two things are very, very different.

Think about it. Your brain's primary job isn't to make you happy, productive, or successful. Its primary job is to keep you alive long enough to pass on your genes. That's it. That's the whole mission. And the way it does that is by constantly scanning your environment for threats. Stephen Porges, the researcher who developed polyvagal theory, calls this process "neuroception" - your nervous system's ability to detect safety and danger without your conscious awareness.

Your neuroception is running 24/7, below the level of your thinking mind. It's reading micro-expressions on faces. It's noticing changes in tone of voice. It's tracking the rhythm of someone's footsteps. It's sensing whether the person walking toward you on the street is a threat or not. And it's doing all of this without you having to think about it.

Now here's where uncertainty comes in. Your nervous system hates ambiguity. It needs clear information to make split-second survival decisions. Is that sound a predator or just the wind? Is that person safe or dangerous? Is this situation familiar or completely unknown?

When your nervous system can't get clear answers, it defaults to one setting: danger. Because from an evolutionary perspective, it's better to mistake a stick for a snake than a snake for a stick. The cost of being wrong about safety is death. The cost of being wrong about danger is just a few wasted seconds of adrenaline.

Wild, right? Your body is literally wired to assume the worst when it doesn't have enough information. And in the modern world, uncertainty is everywhere. It's in the job offer that hasn't come yet. The medical test results you're waiting for. The relationship status that isn't clearly defined. The global pandemic that keeps changing the rules. The news cycle that serves up a fresh crisis every hour.

The body doesn't know the difference between a bear and a breakup

Peter Levine, the creator of Somatic Experiencing, has spent decades studying how the body holds trauma. One of the things he talks about is how your nervous system can't distinguish between a real threat in the present and a memory of a threat from the past. It just reads the signals.

So when you're waiting for a text back from someone you care about, and your stomach drops, and your heart starts racing, and you feel that familiar wave of panic - your body doesn't know that you're just sitting on your couch looking at a phone. It thinks you're in danger. It thinks you need to fight, flee, or freeze to survive.

And here's the part that really gets me. If you grew up in an environment where uncertainty actually was dangerous - where a parent's mood could shift without warning, where love was conditional and unpredictable, where your safety depended on reading every tiny cue correctly - then your nervous system learned a very specific lesson: uncertainty equals threat. Not maybe. Not sometimes. Equals.

That lesson gets encoded in your body at a level deeper than words. It becomes the default operating system. And then you carry that operating system into adulthood, into relationships, into jobs, into every situation where the outcome isn't guaranteed.

Notice what just happened in your body when you read that. Did your shoulders tighten? Did your breath get shallow? Did you feel a familiar ache somewhere in your chest? That's your nervous system responding to the recognition of a pattern it knows all too well.

Bessel van der Kolk, author of "The Body Keeps the Score," showed us that trauma isn't just a story in your head. It's a physiological reality. Your body literally holds the memory of every time uncertainty turned into danger. And it's not going to let you forget.

The two flavors of uncertainty your nervous system can't handle

There are two specific types of uncertainty that tend to trigger nervous system dysregulation, and I think it helps to name them. Because when you can name something, you can start to work with it.

The first is unpredictability. This is when you don't know what's coming next. The timeline is unclear. The outcome is unknown. Your partner is acting distant and you don't know why. Your boss scheduled a meeting with no agenda. You're waiting for test results that could change everything. Your nervous system hates this. It keeps scanning, keeps searching for information, keeps trying to predict what's going to happen so it can prepare. And when it can't find the information it needs, it escalates. More tension. More vigilance. More anxiety.

The second is ambiguity. This is when you can't get a clear read on a situation. Someone says something that could mean multiple things. A social interaction feels off but you can't pinpoint why. You're in a new environment and you don't know the rules yet. Your neuroception is working overtime trying to interpret signals that don't add up to a clear picture. And again, your nervous system defaults to the worst-case interpretation because that's what kept your ancestors alive.

Think about how many times a day you encounter unpredictability and ambiguity. Probably more than you realize. Every notification on your phone is a little dose of uncertainty. Every email that could contain good news or bad. Every social interaction where you have to read between the lines. Every decision about the future that you can't fully control.

No wonder so many of us feel like we're constantly on edge. We're living in a world that floods our nervous systems with uncertainty signals, and most of us never learned how to regulate through that.

Stay with me here, because I know this can feel overwhelming. The point isn't to make you feel hopeless. The point is to help you understand why your reactions make sense. Why your nervous system is doing exactly what it was designed to do. And why the standard advice to "just relax" or "stop worrying so much" doesn't work - because it's asking you to override a survival mechanism with conscious thought, and that's like trying to talk a bear out of hibernating.

What actually helps when your nervous system is screaming

So what do you do when your body is convinced that uncertainty means danger? How do you calm a nervous system that learned, through real experience, that the unknown is something to fear?

A compassionate reframing of trauma that resonates with many is What Happened to You? (paid link) by Bruce Perry and Oprah Winfrey. It shifts the question from "what's wrong with you" to "what happened to you," and that shift alone can change how you relate to your own reactions.

But reading about trauma is only one piece of the puzzle. You also need practical tools for working with your nervous system in real time. A resource that helps make polyvagal theory practical is The Polyvagal Theory in Therapy (paid link) by Deb Dana. She offers concrete exercises for mapping your nervous system states and finding your way back to regulation.

And if you're someone whose body has been holding stress for a long time, you might recognize that chronic tension pattern. A book that connects the dots between stress and illness is When the Body Says No (paid link) by Gabor Mate. He shows how the inability to set boundaries and say no to uncertainty can literally make you sick.

Here's what I've found helpful in my own journey, and I offer it not as advice but as something to try and see if it lands.

First, stop trying to think your way out of nervous system activation. Your thinking brain is not in charge right now. Your survival brain is. So instead of analyzing why you're anxious, turn your attention to your body. Feel your feet on the floor. Notice your breath without trying to change it. Put a hand on your chest or your belly. This isn't about making the anxiety go away. It's about sending a signal to your nervous system that you're present, that you're here, that you're not in immediate danger.

Second, give your nervous system more information. Remember, it's freaking out because it doesn't have enough data. So offer it some. Name where you are. "I'm in my living room. It's 3pm on a Tuesday. I'm wearing a blue sweater. I can hear the refrigerator humming. I'm safe right now in this moment." Your nervous system needs to hear this. Repeatedly. Out loud if you can.

Third, find ways to create predictability in your environment. Your nervous system needs anchors. A consistent morning routine. A familiar playlist. A regular walk. A cup of tea made the same way every day. These small predictable rituals tell your body that some things are reliable, that not everything is uncertain, that there are still patterns you can count on.

Fourth, learn to track your window of tolerance. Dan Siegel coined this term to describe the zone where you can handle life's ups and downs without getting dysregulated. When you're in your window, you can think clearly, feel your emotions without being overwhelmed, and respond rather than react. When you're outside your window, you're in fight, flight, freeze, or collapse. The goal isn't to stay in your window all the time - that's impossible. The goal is to notice when you're leaving it and have tools to bring yourself back.

The uncomfortable truth about certainty

I want to be honest with you about something. The wellness industry loves to sell the idea that you can reach a state of permanent calm. That if you just meditate enough, do enough yoga, take enough supplements, you'll finally be at peace with uncertainty. I don't think that's true.

I think your nervous system will always be alert to the unknown. That's its job. And if you've experienced real trauma, that alert system is going to be more sensitive than other people's. That's not a flaw. That's a sign that your body learned to protect you in a world that wasn't safe.

The goal isn't to eliminate the response to uncertainty. The goal is to build enough capacity in your nervous system that you can feel the activation without being taken over by it. You can feel your heart race and still know you're okay. You can feel the panic rising and still stay present. You can feel the old survival patterns kick in and choose a different response.

A practical guide to working with your nervous system is Anchored (paid link) by Deb Dana. It's full of exercises for building that capacity, for finding your footing when the ground feels shaky, for learning to be with uncertainty without being consumed by it.

I'm not going to tell you that it gets easy. It doesn't. Some days your nervous system will still scream at you over something small. Some days you'll feel like you're back at square one. That's normal. That's part of the process. Recovery isn't linear, and anyone who tells you otherwise is selling something.

But here's what I know from my own experience and from working with others. Over time, with practice, with patience, with the right support and resources, your nervous system can learn new patterns. It can learn that not all uncertainty is danger. It can learn that you can handle the unknown, even when it's uncomfortable. It can learn that you are capable of being with your own fear without being destroyed by it.

That's not a quick fix. That's not a 30-day challenge. That's the slow, messy, beautiful work of rewiring a system that learned to protect you in the only way it knew how.

And honestly? That work is worth doing. Not because you'll ever be perfectly calm. But because you deserve to live a life where uncertainty doesn't run the show. Where you can take risks. Where you can open your heart. Where you can move toward the things you want, even when you don't know how they'll turn out.

Your nervous system was trying to keep you safe. And it did its job. But now you get to teach it something new. You get to show it, over and over again, that you're here. That you're okay. That you can handle what comes.

One breath at a time. One moment at a time. One small act of courage at a time.

Healing Journey Resources

  • What Happened to You? (paid link) - Bruce Perry and Oprah Winfrey offer a compassionate reframing of trauma that shifts the question from "what's wrong with you" to "what happened to you."
  • The Polyvagal Theory in Therapy (paid link) - Deb Dana provides practical exercises for mapping your nervous system states and finding your way back to regulation.
  • When the Body Says No (paid link) - Gabor Mate explores how chronic stress and the inability to set boundaries can lead to illness.
  • Anchored (paid link) - Deb Dana offers a practical guide with exercises for building capacity to work with your nervous system and find safety in uncertainty.

As an Amazon Associate, I earn from qualifying purchases.
